(George Helal Training Center), commonly known as **Ninho do Urubu** ("*The Vulture's Nest*," in [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language")) is the [training ground](/wiki/Training_ground "Training ground") and youth team headquarters of Brazilian [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club [Flamengo](/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo "Clube de Regatas do Flamengo"). It is located in the [Vargem Grande](/wiki/Vargem_Grande,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Vargem Grande, Rio de Janeiro") neighborhood, in the Southwest Zone of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). The construction on the complex began in 2011 and the club began use of the facilities in mid 2012. Between 2015 and 2016, major additions were implemented at the complex including more football pitches, a medical center, and Center of Excellence in Performance (CEP). The total construction cost of the campus is approximately [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")30 million. Construction of the entire complex was completed in 2016.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Association football training ground

Centro de Treinamento George Helal

| Coordinates | [22°59′02.9″S 43°30′20.7″W﻿ / ﻿22.984139°S 43.505750°W﻿ / -22.984139; -43.505750](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Ninho_do_Urubu&params=22_59_02.9_S_43_30_20.7_W_) |

| [Flamengo](/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo "Clube de Regatas do Flamengo")   [Flamengo Youth Academy](/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_Youth_Academy "Clube de Regatas do Flamengo Youth Academy") | |

The **Centro de Treinamento George Helal** (George Helal Training Center), commonly known as **Ninho do Urubu** ("*The Vulture's Nest*," in [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language")) is the [training ground](/wiki/Training_ground "Training ground") and youth team headquarters of Brazilian [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club [Flamengo](/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo "Clube de Regatas do Flamengo"). It is located in the [Vargem Grande](/wiki/Vargem_Grande,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Vargem Grande, Rio de Janeiro") neighborhood, in the Southwest Zone of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). The construction on the complex began in 2011 and the club began use of the facilities in mid 2012. Between 2015 and 2016, major additions were implemented at the complex including more football pitches, a medical center, and Center of Excellence in Performance (CEP). The total construction cost of the campus is approximately [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")30 million. Construction of the entire complex was completed in 2016.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

On the morning of February 8, 2019 a fire broke out in the living quarters of several youth academy players while they were sleeping. Ten players between the ages of 14 and 17 were killed, and three others were hospitalized with burn injuries.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ninho_do_Urubu&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The property where the complex is located was acquired on August 30, 1984 by president [George Helal](/w/index.php?title=George_Helal&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Helal (page does not exist)"), but the start of construction was delayed for many years. At the time, the land was purchased for Cr$300 million ([Brazilian cruzeiro](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_cruzeiro_(1970%E2%80%931986)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brazilian cruzeiro (1970–1986) (page does not exist)")). The real estate was later valued at [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")350 million, an increase of 6,900 %. On May 21, 2011 under the management of club president [Patricia Amorim](/wiki/Patricia_Amorim "Patricia Amorim"), the site was authorized for construction. The total land area is 140,000 m².

## Facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ninho_do_Urubu&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Facilities")]

### Press room

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ninho_do_Urubu&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Press room")]

On 30 November 2016 after the [LaMia Airlines Flight 2933](/wiki/LaMia_Airlines_Flight_2933 "LaMia Airlines Flight 2933") airplane crash, Flamengo announced that would name the Ninho do Urubu press room after [Victorino Chermont](/wiki/Victorino_Chermont "Victorino Chermont"). Victorino, one of the victims of the crash, was a journalist of [Fox Sports Brazil](/wiki/Fox_Sports "Fox Sports") who had worked for many years covering Flamengo and was a known club supporter.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## 2016 Summer Olympics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ninho_do_Urubu&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2016 Summer Olympics")]

During [Rio 2016](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") the facilities were used by [Brazil U23](/wiki/Brazil_national_under-23_football_team "Brazil national under-23 football team") and [Argentina U23](/wiki/Argentina_national_under-23_football_team "Argentina national under-23 football team") football teams.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The agreement to use the space was signed between Flamengo and the Rio 2016 Organizing Committee.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## February 8, 2019 fire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ninho_do_Urubu&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: February 8, 2019 fire")]

Main article: [Ninho do Urubu fire](/wiki/Ninho_do_Urubu_fire "Ninho do Urubu fire")

On the morning of February 8, 2019, a fire erupted at the Ninho do Urubu living quarters.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The fire resulted in the deaths of ten academy players between the ages of 14 and 17 training with the club. Three others were injured. The cause of the fire was a malfunctioning [air-conditioning unit](/wiki/Air_conditioning "Air conditioning") that caught fire in the room of one of the victims close to 5:00. The location of the fire was the temporary living quarters in a newly expanded section of the campus. The state Labor Ministry launched a task force to determine if any preventative actions could have been taken, and to ensure that the families of the victims would be accommodated.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The victims of the fire were Athila de Souza Paixão (14), Arthur Vinícius de Barros da Silva Freitas (14), Bernardo Pisetta (14), Christian Esmério Candido (15), Jorge Eduardo dos Santos Ferreira Sacramento (15), Pablo Henrique da Silva Matos (14), Vitor Isaías (15), Samuel Thomas de Souza Rosa (15), Gerdson Santos (14), and Rykelmo de Souza Viana (17).

Cauan Emanuel Gomes Nunes (14 years old), Francisco Dyogo Bento Alves (15), and Jhonatha Cruz Ventura (15) were hospitalized with injuries; Jhonatha's condition was the most severe.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Club president [Rodolfo Landim](/wiki/Rodolfo_Landim "Rodolfo Landim") described it as "the worst tragedy the club has ever experienced in its 123 years."[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [governor](/wiki/Wilson_Witzel "Wilson Witzel") of the state of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)") declared a three-day period of mourning following the tragedy.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ninho_do_Urubu&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: External links")]

* [Official website](http://www.flamengo.com.br)
